Specifically, array-pose rotation improves inter-user channel orthogonality even with isotropic elements, whereas directional elements introduce a tradeoff between phase-based spatial separation and boresight-dependent gain alignment. Motivated by these insights, we develop an efficient optimization framework that jointly coordinates transmit beamforming, array-pose adaptation, and element-boresight steering to exploit the geometry-induced phase-and-gain channel-shaping capability. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed joint design outperforms fixed-array, pose-only, and boresight-only benchmarks, with larger gains achieved under more directive element patterns and tighter boresight-steering constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a geometry-reconfigurable transmission system using a rotatable antenna array, jointly optimizing transmit beamformers, array pose, and element boresights to maximize weighted sum-rate. The design outperforms fixed-array and partial-reconfiguration baselines, especially with directive elements and tight steering constraints.
AI.motivation: To go beyond conventional fixed antenna arrays by exploiting both array-pose adjustment and element-level boresight steering, enabling dynamic reshaping of phase responses and channel gains for improved multiuser communication.
AI.method: Formulates a weighted sum-rate maximization problem jointly optimizing beamformers, array pose, and element boresights. Provides geometric interpretation via zero-forcing analysis, then develops an efficient optimization framework coordinating all three design variables.
AI.result: The proposed joint design outperforms fixed-array, pose-only, and boresight-only benchmarks, with larger gains observed under more directive element patterns and tighter boresight-steering constraints.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-reconfigurable transmission via rotatable antenna arrays significantly enhances multiuser communication performance by jointly exploiting channel-strength enhancement and spatial-separability improvement, with the approach being most beneficial under directive elements and constrained steering.

title: How Much Future Helps? A Controlled Study of Future-Privileged Supervision for Causal Egocentric Gaze Estimation
comment: Accepted to the 7th International Workshop on Eye and Gaze in Computer Vision (GAZE 2026), CVPR 2026. Best Paper Award
summary: Egocentric gaze estimation is commonly studied using models that process the full video with access to future frames, while real-world applications require strictly causal, online prediction. This discrepancy raises key questions: Does future context inherently provide valuable signals for gaze estimation? If so, how much future look-ahead optimally supervises a causal model during training? To investigate, we propose a controlled framework featuring a future-aware branch that accesses a tunable look-ahead horizon during training but is discarded at inference. This design isolates the impact of future context while keeping the inference architecture fixed and strictly causal. Across EGTEA Gaze+ and Ego4D, we find that future-privileged supervision consistently improves causal gaze prediction, confirming its utility. However, performance gains do not increase monotonically with longer look-ahead, but rather peak within a bounded temporal regime. Specifically, optimal performance corresponds to roughly 1.7--3.3 seconds of future context ($H{\in}[5, 10]$) on EGTEA Gaze+ and 2.7 seconds ($H{=}10$) on Ego4D. Our results demonstrate that lightweight causal models can effectively absorb future-aware signals, providing practical guidance for real-time egocentric gaze modeling.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether future context improves egocentric gaze estimation for causal (online) models, finding that future-privileged supervision during training boosts performance, with optimal look-ahead around 1.7-3.3 seconds, beyond which gains plateau.
AI.motivation: Standard egocentric gaze estimation models use full video with future frames, but real-world applications require causal, online prediction. The authors investigate whether future context provides valuable signals and how much look-ahead is optimal for training causal models.
AI.method: A controlled framework with a future-aware branch that accesses a tunable look-ahead horizon during training (but discarded at inference), keeping the inference architecture strictly causal and fixed.
AI.result: Future-privileged supervision consistently improves causal gaze prediction. Optimal performance peaks with bounded look-ahead: ~1.7-3.3 seconds (H∈[5,10]) on EGTEA Gaze+ and ~2.7 seconds (H=10) on Ego4D. Gains do not increase monotonically with longer horizons.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight causal models can effectively absorb future-aware signals, providing practical guidance for real-time egocentric gaze modeling.

title: Path planning for unmanned naval surface vehicles
summary: There nowadays is a myriad of approaches to real-time avoidance of fixed obstacles for unmanned surface vehicles (USVs) and, to a lesser extent, also the task of avoiding moving obstacles such as boats, ships, swimmers, and other USVs, but both topics still present challenges. This paper offers novel approaches to both of these problems. It uses a combination of a global path planner, which finds a path from a start point to a goal point that avoids fixed obstacles (given that their locations are known in advance), and a local path planner, which can circumnavigate a moving obstacle (as well as any previously unknown fixed obstacles). The global planner is novel in that it employs a combination of three path planners, one known in the literature as Grassfire, one that is a new modification of Grassfire, and one that is a new, and arguably more intuitive, version of the well-known Probabilistic Roadmap. The local planner is novel in that it employs a higher-level decision logic based on its observations regarding the direction of movement of the obstacle relative to the USVs global path. This logic enables the USV to determine the best strategy for avoiding the obstacle by systematically routing the vehicle behind the obstacle rather than running parallel to it until the opportunity to pass appears. Simulations are provided that validate these claims. For comparison with other systems, the simulations include an implementation of the well-known D* algorithm, and the discussion covers additional dynamic path planning systems, which, like D*, do not necessarily route the vehicle behind the moving obstacle.

AI.tldr: This paper presents novel global and local path planning approaches for USVs to avoid both fixed and moving obstacles, using a combination of three global planners (Grassfire, modified Grassfire, and new PRM variant) and a local planner with higher-level decision logic that routes the vehicle behind moving obstacles.
AI.motivation: Despite many existing approaches for real-time obstacle avoidance for USVs, both fixed and moving obstacle avoidance remain challenging problems.
AI.method: Combines a global path planner (using Grassfire, modified Grassfire, and a new Probabilistic Roadmap variant) with a local planner that employs decision logic based on obstacle movement direction relative to the USV's global path.
AI.result: Simulations validate the claims, with comparisons against the D* algorithm and other dynamic planning systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach systematically routes the USV behind moving obstacles, offering advantages over methods that run parallel to obstacles.

title: Multi-modal Rail Crossing Safety Analysis
summary: Given one or more images of a railway crossing, can we leverage visual cues that allow us to robustly estimate how safe it is? Can we improve our ability to do so by introducing structured data (such as official accident reports) about the accident history of that crossing into our models? In this work, we explore how to best answer those questions towards building an AI system that can ingest multi-modal data for railway crossings and provide safety assessment and scores that align with expert opinion and with safety scoring used by the Federal Railroad Administration (FRA). To that end, we propose a proof-of-concept pipeline that delivers on that goal, while at the same time exploring and tackling a number of critical research challenges that pertain to different parts of the pipeline, from data preparation to different learning paradigms that can allow us to realize such a system. Indicatively, our proposed system identifies HIGH-RISK and LOW-RISK crossings with a macro F1 score of 0.757 and estimates FRA-based safety scores with an RMSE of 0.078 and correlation of 0.492 using a routed fine-tuned compact VLM pipeline, while producing qualitative results that align with domain-expert assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-modal AI pipeline that combines railway crossing images with structured accident report data to assess crossing safety, achieving strong alignment with expert opinion and FRA safety scoring standards.
AI.motivation: To determine if visual cues from railway crossing images can robustly estimate safety, and whether incorporating structured accident report data improves safety assessment, toward building an AI system aligned with expert opinion and FRA scoring.
AI.method: A proof-of-concept multi-modal pipeline using a routed fine-tuned compact Vision-Language Model (VLM) that ingests both images of railway crossings and structured accident report data to produce safety assessments and scores.
AI.result: The system achieves macro F1 of 0.757 for HIGH/LOW-RISK classification, RMSE of 0.078 and correlation of 0.492 for FRA-based safety score estimation, with qualitative results matching domain-expert assessment.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal data (images + structured accident reports) enables robust railway crossing safety assessment, and the proposed pipeline demonstrates feasibility for aligning AI-driven safety scoring with expert and regulatory standards.

title: Procedural Memory Distillation: Online Reflection for Self-Improving Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), along with recent selfdistillation variants such as SDPO, evaluates each rollout against a verifier and updates the policy from that episode-level signal. However, the richer procedural information in the rollout is rarely retained or reused. Across episodes and epochs, the model repeatedly encounters related problems under a changing policy, producing cross-episode signals that episode-local updates cannot capture: which strategies consistently pass verification, which failure modes persist, which patterns recur. We propose Procedural Memory Distillation (PMD), which converts these crossepisode signals into reusable procedural memory and distills it into the policy's weights during training. This memory functions as a training scaffold, absorbed into the policy itself, yielding a memory-free model at inference. PMD organizes the memory at three levels of abstraction: raw trajectories, self-reflected strategies and lessons, and higher-level behavioral patterns that recur across problems, all extracted online from the model's own trajectories. A memory-conditioned self-teacher draws on the accumulated experience to supervise the student on its own rollouts, enabling student to progressively internalize procedural knowledge within its parameters. The central design principle is co-evolution: the policy generates rollouts that update the memory, and memory shapes the supervision that updates the policy. Empirically, across Qwen3-8B and OLMo3-Instruct-7B, PMD improves over SDPO by 3.8-5.5% on SCIKNOWEVAL and 7.9-13.6% on LIVECODEBENCH. Co-evolution powers these gains: freezing either the memory or the policy trails PMD by more than 10% across SCIKNOWEVAL domains.

AI.tldr: PMD improves RLVR by storing cross-episode procedural memory (trajectories, strategies, behavioral patterns) and distilling it back into the policy via a memory-conditioned self-teacher, achieving 3.8-13.6% gains over SDPO on reasoning and code benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard RLVR and SDPO only use episode-level verification signals, discarding rich procedural information across episodes and epochs—such as which strategies pass verification, which failures persist, and which patterns recur—that could improve learning.
AI.method: PMD constructs procedural memory at three abstraction levels (raw trajectories, self-reflected strategies/lessons, recurring behavioral patterns) from the model's own rollouts online. A memory-conditioned self-teacher supervises the student policy using this accumulated experience, enabling co-evolution where policy updates memory and memory shapes policy updates.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B and OLMo3-Instruct-7B, PMD outperforms SDPO by 3.8-5.5% on SCIKNOWEVAL and 7.9-13.6% on LIVECODEBENCH. Ablation shows freezing either memory or policy leads to >10% degradation across SCIKNOWEVAL domains.
AI.conclusion: Cross-episode procedural memory distillation with co-evolution significantly improves policy learning over single-episode RLVR methods, and the memory-free inference model retains the benefits without extra overhead.

title: Robust Transmission Design for RIS-Assisted RSMA-SWIPT Systems With Movable Antennas Under Hardware Distortions
summary: This paper investigates a robust transmission design for a multi-user rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA)-based simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT) system empowered by movable antennas (MAs) and a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) under channel state information (CSI) uncertainty and residual hardware impairments (HIs). The effective channels in MAs-enabled systems depend on antenna positions, causing CSI uncertainty to affect not only active and passive beamforming but also antenna position optimization. Furthermore, residual HIs distort the effective SINRs, creating additional coupling among beamforming, RIS reflection control, common-rate allocation, power-splitting ratio optimization, and antenna position optimization. Consequently, the joint impact of CSI uncertainty and HIs leads to a highly coupled and challenging resource allocation problem. To address this challenge, we propose a robust resource allocation framework that jointly optimizes common-rate allocation, transmit beamforming, RIS reflection coefficients, power-splitting ratios, and MAs positions to maximize the achievable sum-rate while satisfying practical system constraints. To obtain an efficient solution, the original problem is decomposed into active beamforming, RIS reflection design, power-splitting ratio optimization, and MAs position optimization subproblems, where tractable convex surrogate functions are constructed to handle the non-convex objective and constraints. Simulation results verify the effectiveness of the proposed framework and demonstrate substantial improvements in achievable sum-rate, robustness against CSI uncertainty and hardware impairments, and convergence performance compared with benchmark schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a robust resource allocation framework for RSMA-based SWIPT systems with movable antennas and RIS, jointly optimizing beamforming, RIS coefficients, power-splitting ratios, and antenna positions under CSI uncertainty and hardware impairments to maximize sum-rate.
AI.motivation: To address the highly coupled resource allocation problem caused by joint impact of CSI uncertainty and residual hardware impairments in MAs/RIS-enabled RSMA-SWIPT systems, where CSI uncertainty affects not only beamforming but also antenna positions.
AI.method: Decomposes the original problem into active beamforming, RIS reflection design, power-splitting ratio optimization, and MAs position optimization subproblems; uses tractable convex surrogate functions to handle non-convex objectives and constraints.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate substantial improvements in achievable sum-rate, robustness against CSI uncertainty and hardware impairments, and convergence performance compared with benchmark schemes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed robust framework effectively addresses joint uncertainties and impairments in MAs/RIS-enabled RSMA-SWIPT systems, achieving superior performance and robustness.

title: A Cost-Aware, Paired Protocol for Auditing Dynamic Tool Synthesis in Agentic Video Question Answering
summary: Agentic Video Question Answering (VideoQA) systems invoke tools during inference, but their tool libraries are fixed, so recurring procedures are rebuilt from primitives on every question. Synthesizing composite tools could remove this overhead, but whether such expansion helps is hard to assess: final-answer accuracy, the standard metric, ignores inference effort, so it cannot reveal how a system shifts cost. We propose a cost-aware, paired protocol for auditing tool-augmented video agents. The protocol pairs two complete systems on the same input for each question and reports their net difference across accuracy and cost jointly. For each question, it sorts the paired outcome into one of six groups defined by joint correctness and by the change in visible tool calls, separating accuracy-preserving efficiency gains from harmful regressions. Significance is reported with McNemar's test and paired bootstrap confidence intervals. We instantiate the protocol on Dynamic-SAGE, an agentic VideoQA framework that synthesizes, validates, and persistently registers executable composite tools for reuse on unseen questions, and evaluate it against the SAGE baseline on SAGE-Bench. The audit reveals a multi-axis profile that a scalar accuracy comparison would miss: Dynamic-SAGE improves accuracy by 7.5 points (p < 0.001) and reduces reasoning turns and visible tool calls by roughly 28%, while shifting rather than reducing inference cost, as token usage rises 34% and cost 26%. Gains are largest on visual and open-ended questions and neutral on verbal and multimodal ones, and residual failures concentrate on hard, open-ended questions where the pipeline does the most work. By measuring accuracy and cost jointly, the protocol shows where the pipeline-level difference is reliable and where it is not. The code is available at https://github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/Dynamic-SAGE.

AI.tldr: A paper proposing Dynamic-SAGE, an agentic VideoQA system that synthesizes reusable composite tools, and a cost-aware paired audit protocol that jointly evaluates accuracy and inference cost, revealing efficiency gains (7.5 accuracy points, 28% fewer tool calls) alongside increased token usage and cost that scalar metrics would miss.
AI.motivation: Standard VideoQA systems use fixed tool libraries and rebuild procedures from scratch for each question, wasting inference effort. Moreover, accuracy-only metrics cannot capture cost shifts, making it hard to assess whether tool synthesis actually helps.
AI.method: (1) Dynamic-SAGE: an agentic VideoQA framework that synthesizes, validates, and persistently registers composite tools for reuse on unseen questions. (2) A cost-aware paired audit protocol that compares two complete systems side-by-side, sorting outcomes into six groups by joint correctness and change in visible tool calls, with McNemar's test and paired bootstrap confidence intervals.
AI.result: Dynamic-SAGE improves accuracy by 7.5 points (p < 0.001), reduces reasoning turns and visible tool calls by ~28%, but increases token usage by 34% and cost by 26%. Gains concentrate on visual and open-ended questions; failures persist on hard, open-ended questions.
AI.conclusion: The joint accuracy-cost protocol reveals a nuanced profile that scalar accuracy metrics would miss, showing where pipeline-level differences are reliable and where they are not. Dynamic-SAGE demonstrates that composite tool synthesis can improve efficiency without sacrificing accuracy, though inference cost shifts rather than disappears.

title: One Demonstration Is Enough for Real-World Robotic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Learning effective robot control policies on physical hardware is challenging due to costly data collection and the difficulty of reward specification. Prior work has incorporated demonstrations into reinforcement learning (RL), yet existing approaches either require large numbers of demonstrations or depend on continuous human intervention during training. To address these limitations, we present AutoSERL, a framework that leverages a single demonstration to fully automate the intervention process in real-world robot RL. The framework includes three complementary mechanisms to accomplish certain tasks: a sliding window intervention mechanism that continuously guides exploration to prevent local optima and unsafe deviations, a safety recovery mechanism that detects and corrects failure states via predefined trajectory recovery points, and an intervention termination criterion that automatically disables guidance once the policy can independently complete the task, preserving its exploration advantage. We evaluate AutoSERL on six contact-intensive manipulation tasks across two robot platforms, spanning insertion, hanging, and hinge-based tasks. AutoSERL consistently outperforms SERL initialized with 20 demonstrations, behavior cloning, and MILES -- a dedicated one-shot imitation learning baseline -- across all tasks while matching HIL-SERL, achieves 100% success rate on insertion tasks, and demonstrates improved robustness to positional variations, all from a single demonstration. Code and videos are available on our project website: https://autoserl.github.io/.

AI.tldr: AutoSERL is a framework that uses just one demonstration to automate real-world robot reinforcement learning, outperforming many baselines on contact-intensive manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Robot RL on physical hardware is costly due to data collection and reward specification. Existing methods need many demos or constant human intervention. The authors aim to reduce these requirements.
AI.method: Three mechanisms: (1) sliding window intervention for guiding exploration, (2) safety recovery mechanism for detecting/correcting failures, (3) intervention termination criterion that auto-disables guidance when policy is competent.
AI.result: Outperforms SERL (20 demos), behavior cloning, and MILES on six tasks across two platforms; matches HIL-SERL; 100% success on insertion tasks; robust to positional variations.
AI.conclusion: A single demonstration can effectively automate intervention in real-world robot RL, achieving competitive or superior results to methods requiring many more demonstrations or constant human oversight.

title: How Should Transformers Encode Numeric Values in Electronic Health Records?
comment: 16 pages, 15 figures, 3 tables, accepted to ICML 2026, to be published in Proceedings of Machine Learning Research
summary: How do we encode numeric values in transformer-based sequence processing, particularly in electronic health record (EHR) data? We systematically compare discrete, continuous, and hybrid value encoding strategies using synthetic arithmetic tasks embedded within real-world EHR data, as well as real-world clinical prediction tasks. Our study reveals trade-offs between numeric precision, optimisation stability, and architectural flexibility. We find that approaches that explicitly model value-concept interactions perform best on precision-sensitive arithmetic tasks when architectural constraints permit. Hybrid token-based approaches that retain numeric values but apply binning prior to projection provide a more robust and broadly applicable alternative, with the optimal number of bins following a simple empirically derived power-law in dataset size. Across tasks, models consistently exhibit reliable "good enough" numeric computation rather than exact arithmetic, while clinical gains from incorporating laboratory values are task-dependent. This suggests that robustness and deployability often outweigh maximal numeric precision in practice, motivating hybrid token-based approaches as a practical default.

AI.tldr: This paper compares discrete, continuous, and hybrid numeric encoding strategies in transformer-based models for EHR data, finding hybrid token-based approaches with binning offer the best practical trade-off between precision and robustness, with optimal bin count following a power-law based on dataset size.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare how numeric values should be encoded in transformer-based sequence processing for EHR data, addressing the trade-offs between precision, stability, and architectural flexibility.
AI.method: Compared discrete, continuous, and hybrid value encoding strategies using synthetic arithmetic tasks embedded in real EHR data and real-world clinical prediction tasks; analyzed value-concept interaction modeling, binning strategies, and power-law relationships.
AI.result: Value-concept interaction methods excel at precision-sensitive arithmetic under suitable architectures; hybrid token-based binning is more robust and broadly applicable; optimal bin count follows a power-law in dataset size; models exhibit "good enough" computation rather than exact arithmetic; clinical gains from lab values are task-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid token-based approaches are recommended as a practical default for numeric encoding in EHR transformers, as robustness and deployability often outweigh maximal numeric precision.

title: The Agentic Garden of Forking Paths
summary: Empirical research rarely admits a unique analysis. Different analytical choices can lead to different conclusions from the same data, yet these hidden forking paths are difficult to observe. We show that AI agents capture much of the analytical variation among human researchers while making these paths explicit. Across four high-stakes domains, assigning different personas is sufficient for AI agents to report divergent, often opposing, conclusions from the same data and question, with findings systematically aligned with those beliefs. In a study in which 42 human research teams analyzed the same immigration dataset, AI agents reproduced 72% of the human ideological gap in reported effect estimates. Despite reaching opposing conclusions, it is difficult to identify clear issues in each analysis based on the final AI reports: 86% passed independent AI review and 78% passed majority human expert review. These findings suggest that the central challenge is often not flawed analyses, but selective exploration and reporting from a large space of methodologically defensible analyses. AI agents may amplify this longstanding problem by making such exploration inexpensive and scalable. To address this, we introduce the m-value (multiverse value), the probability that an analysis path would produce a claim at least as extreme as the reported one. We further introduce Agentic Bootstrap, which estimates the m-value by using AI agents to sample plausible analysis paths. Applied to the human immigration study, 13.5% of reported human analyses fell in the most extreme 5% of the analysis space (m<0.05). Scientific evidence should therefore be evaluated not only by a single reported analysis but also by its position within the distribution of analyses that could reasonably have been reported. Agentic Bootstrap makes this distribution observable and turns it into a criterion for scientific credibility.

AI.tldr: AI agents can reproduce human-like analytical variation, leading to divergent conclusions from the same data. The paper introduces m-value and Agentic Bootstrap to quantify how extreme a given analysis is within the space of plausible analyses.
AI.motivation: Empirical research rarely has a unique analysis; different choices yield different conclusions. These "forking paths" are hidden, and the authors aim to make them observable and quantifiable.
AI.method: AI agents with different personas analyzed same datasets across four domains. In an immigration study with 42 human teams, AI agents replicated 72% of the human ideological gap. The authors propose m-value (probability a random analysis path yields a claim at least as extreme) and Agentic Bootstrap (using AI to sample plausible analysis paths).
AI.result: AI agents reproduced 72% of human ideological gaps. 86% of AI reports passed independent AI review; 78% passed human expert review. 13.5% of reported human analyses fell in the most extreme 5% of the analysis space (m<0.05).
AI.conclusion: The core problem is selective exploration/reporting from a large space of defensible analyses. Scientific evidence should be evaluated by its position within the distribution of reasonable analyses. Agentic Bootstrap makes this observable.

title: LLM-Empowered Multimodal Fusion Framework for Autonomous Driving: Semantic Enhancement and Channel-Adaptive Design
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Accepted by 2026 IEEE 37th International Symposium on Personal, Indoor and Mobile Radio Communications (PIMRC)
summary: Vision-radar fusion is central to robust autonomous driving, combining dense visual semantics with precise range and velocity measurements from radar. However, real-world fusion quality is fundamentally challenged by dynamically varying input quality, stemming from occlusion, adverse weather, and channel noise. To address this, we re-frame the problem from static data fusion to channel-aware semantic reasoning and propose a Large Language Model-centric Semantic-layer Channel-aware Integrated Perception (LM-SCIP) framework. It places a Large Language Model (LLM) as a central reasoning core to fuse a local visual stream with a quality-varying external radar stream used to cover perception-blind spots. Concretely, LM-SCIP couples a hierarchical radar-vision encoder with a Channel-Adaptive Semantic Module (CASM) that maps link indicators into a "Channel Prompt" to dynamically gate external radar features. A parameter-efficient, LoRA-tuned LLM, in conjunction with a heterogeneous Mixture-of-Experts (H-MoE), then arbitrates between local visual cues and the channel-conditioned radar context. Finally, a decoupled multi-task decoder outputs localization, trajectory forecasting, and image reconstruction. Experiments on nuScenes and VIRAT validate our approach. On nuScenes, under a controlled toggle of radar input, LM-SCIP reduces localization RMSE by 40.0% versus a vision-only baseline. On VIRAT, the model attains a 0.214m localization RMSE and 0.179m minFDE (k=1). These results reveal that the proposed LM-SCIP enables a robust vision-dominant fallback at low SNR and synergistic fusion at high SNR.

AI.tldr: LM-SCIP proposes an LLM-centric framework for vision-radar fusion that uses a Channel-Adaptive Semantic Module and heterogeneous Mixture-of-Experts to dynamically adapt fusion based on radar signal quality, achieving robust performance across varying channel conditions.
AI.motivation: Real-world vision-radar fusion suffers from dynamically varying input quality due to occlusion, adverse weather, and channel noise, making static fusion approaches inadequate.
AI.method: LM-SCIP places an LLM as central reasoning core, couples hierarchical radar-vision encoder with Channel-Adaptive Semantic Module (CASM) generating "Channel Prompt", uses LoRA-tuned LLM with heterogeneous Mixture-of-Experts (H-MoE) for arbitration, and outputs via decoupled multi-task decoder.
AI.result: On nuScenes, LM-SCIP reduces localization RMSE by 40.0% vs vision-only baseline under controlled radar toggle. On VIRAT, achieves 0.214m localization RMSE and 0.179m minFDE (k=1).
AI.conclusion: LM-SCIP enables robust vision-dominant fallback at low SNR and synergistic fusion at high SNR, demonstrating effective channel-aware dynamic fusion for autonomous driving.

title: Anti-Prompt: Image Protection against Text-Guided Image-to-Video Generation
summary: Recent advances in Image-to-Video generation allow a single image to be animated into a convincing video under text guidance, raising serious copyright and privacy risks. We propose Anti-Prompt, an image protection approach that injects imperceptible perturbations into an image, inducing visible inconsistencies and structural failures in text-guided I2V generation. Our method is motivated by a simple empirical observation. When text guidance is removed from modern I2V models, generation quality degrades markedly, not only in motion realism but also in subject preservation, structural coherence, and temporal consistency. Building on this insight, Anti-Prompt exploits the model reliance on textual guidance by attenuating text-conditioned interactions during denoising while strengthening visual-only pathways. To further systematically evaluate protection effectiveness, we introduce a Video-LLM-assisted evaluation protocol that provides interpretable, frame-grounded analyses of generation artifacts and inconsistencies. Experiments on two representative I2V architectures demonstrate that our method achieves strong protection performance while improving efficiency and cross-model transferability.

AI.tldr: Anti-Prompt protects images by injecting imperceptible perturbations that cause visible inconsistencies in text-guided Image-to-Video generation, exploiting the model's reliance on textual guidance by attenuating text-conditioned interactions.
AI.motivation: To address copyright and privacy risks from recent advances in Image-to-Video generation that can animate a single image into convincing videos under text guidance.
AI.method: Injecting imperceptible perturbations that attenuate text-conditioned interactions during denoising while strengthening visual-only pathways, based on the observation that removing text guidance degrades generation quality.
AI.result: Achieves strong protection performance on two representative I2V architectures with improved efficiency and cross-model transferability.
AI.conclusion: Anti-Prompt is an effective image protection method against unauthorized text-guided I2V generation, with a proposed Video-LLM-assisted evaluation protocol for interpretable analysis.

title: Imagining the Sense of Touch: Touch-Informed Manipulation via Imagined Tactile Representations
summary: Tactile sensing can substantially improve contact-rich robotic manipulation, yet its practical deployment remains limited by the fragility, calibration requirements, and maintenance burden of tactile hardware. This raises a fundamental question: can robots benefit from tactile knowledge without requiring tactile sensors at deployment? We present TacImag, a tactile imagination framework that predicts tactile observations from vision and proprioception and uses the generated signals to guide manipulation policies. Trained from paired visuotactile demonstrations, TacImag enables touch-informed manipulation using only visual observations at test time. We evaluate TacImag on six simulated and four real-world manipulation tasks. Across simulation and real-world experiments, imagined tactile observations consistently improve manipulation performance without requiring tactile hardware. In real-world experiments, imagined force fields improve contact-sensitive tasks by 44.4% on average, whereas imagined tactile images improve texture-sensitive tasks by 23.3%, revealing that the effectiveness of tactile imagination depends strongly on the relationship between tactile representation and task requirements. Our results further suggest that tactile imagination does not simply recover missing tactile measurements. Instead, it acts as a form of contact-aware supervision that transforms subtle visual interaction cues into representations that are easier for manipulation policies to exploit.

AI.tldr: TacImag predicts tactile signals from vision/proprioception without physical tactile sensors at deployment, improving manipulation performance across simulation and real-world tasks by 23-44%.
AI.motivation: Tactile sensors are fragile, require calibration, and add maintenance burden, limiting their practical deployment. The key question is whether robots can benefit from tactile knowledge without needing tactile sensors at test time.
AI.method: TacImag is a tactile imagination framework trained on paired visuotactile demonstrations. It predicts tactile observations (force fields or tactile images) from vision and proprioception, then uses these generated signals to guide manipulation policies at test time using only visual observations.
AI.result: Imagined force fields improved contact-sensitive tasks by 44.4%, and imagined tactile images improved texture-sensitive tasks by 23.3% in real-world experiments. Tactile imagination acts as contact-aware supervision transforming visual cues into exploitable representations.
AI.conclusion: Tactile imagination enables touch-informed manipulation without tactile hardware at deployment, with effectiveness depending on the alignment between tactile representation and task requirements. It serves as contact-aware supervision rather than merely recovering missing tactile data.

title: NeuroBridge: Bridging Multi-Task MRI Knowledge for Neurodegenerative Disease Diagnosis
summary: INTRODUCTION: Accurate MRI-based identification of Alzheimer's disease (AD), mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and related dementias remains challenging because disease-related structural changes are often subtle and heterogeneous. We developed NeuroBridge, a clinically guided multi-task MRI framework for neurodegenerative disease diagnosis. METHODS: NeuroBridge integrates large-scale self-supervised MRI pretraining with hippocampal segmentation, hippocampal atrophy classification, and reconstruction objectives, followed by gated fusion fine-tuning. Performance was evaluated across ADNI and OASIS cohorts, including cross-cohort transfer, probability-based analysis, and opportunistic screening. RESULTS: NeuroBridge achieved the highest performance across evaluated classification tasks, reaching 88.17% accuracy for AD versus cognitively normal controls in ADNI and 82.78% in OASIS. The largest gains occurred in MCI-related and mixed-diagnosis settings. The framework demonstrated strong cross-cohort generalization, systematic associations between predicted-class probability and accuracy, and the feasibility of probability-based opportunistic screening. DISCUSSION: Clinically guided multi-task representation learning improves neurodegenerative MRI diagnosis beyond conventional single-task approaches. NeuroBridge provides a robust and scalable framework for dementia assessment and MRI-based opportunistic screening.

AI.tldr: NeuroBridge is a clinically guided multi-task MRI framework that integrates self-supervised pretraining with hippocampal segmentation, atrophy classification, and reconstruction, achieving high accuracy (88.17% for AD vs controls) and strong cross-cohort generalization for Alzheimer's disease diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Accurate MRI-based identification of Alzheimer's disease, MCI, and related dementias remains challenging because disease-related structural changes are often subtle and heterogeneous, motivating the need for a more robust framework.
AI.method: NeuroBridge integrates large-scale self-supervised MRI pretraining with hippocampal segmentation, hippocampal atrophy classification, and reconstruction objectives, followed by gated fusion fine-tuning.
AI.result: Achieved 88.17% accuracy for AD vs controls in ADNI and 82.78% in OASIS; largest gains in MCI-related and mixed-diagnosis settings; demonstrated strong cross-cohort generalization and feasibility of probability-based opportunistic screening.
AI.conclusion: Clinically guided multi-task representation learning improves neurodegenerative MRI diagnosis beyond conventional single-task approaches, providing a robust and scalable framework for dementia assessment and opportunistic screening.

title: Janus: a Playground for User-Involved Agentic Permission Management
comment: Code and data released on GitHub: https://github.com/GraceBrigham/Janus
summary: AI agents that autonomously execute tool calls on a user's behalf raise pressing questions about permission management: what role could users play, and what role should they play? Despite many proposed approaches, the user's role in agentic permission management remains under explored. We introduce Janus, a playground system for implementing and evaluating user-involved agentic permission management designs. Janus consists of two components: Janus-Core, a modular agentic system supporting a diverse spectrum of permission management designs, and Janus-Harness, an automated evaluation framework. Grounded in a conceptual model that identifies key design axes for user involvement, we implement six permission assistants spanning the design space and evaluate them across three scenarios and three synthetic responders. We demonstrate that user input is critical and can significantly strengthen privacy and security, that AI augmentation of user decisions can help reduce cognitive load, and that realistic user behavior including permission fatigue must be accounted for in system design. No single design performs optimally across all contexts, motivating a more principled and context-sensitive approach to deploying permission assistants in agentic systems. Janus is publicly available to support future investigation into this dimension of agentic system design.

AI.tldr: Janus is a playground system for implementing and evaluating user-involved permission management designs in AI agentic systems, demonstrating that user input is critical for privacy/security, AI augmentation reduces cognitive load, and permission fatigue must be accounted for—with no single design universally optimal.
AI.motivation: The user's role in agentic permission management remains under explored despite many proposed approaches, and there is a need to systematically understand what role users could and should play when AI agents autonomously execute tool calls on their behalf.
AI.method: The authors built Janus with two components: Janus-Core (a modular agentic system supporting diverse permission management designs) and Janus-Harness (an automated evaluation framework). They implemented six permission assistants spanning the design space, grounded in a conceptual model identifying key design axes for user involvement, and evaluated them across three scenarios and three synthetic responders.
AI.result: User input is critical and can significantly strengthen privacy and security; AI augmentation of user decisions can help reduce cognitive load; realistic user behavior including permission fatigue must be accounted for; no single design performs optimally across all contexts.
AI.conclusion: A more principled and context-sensitive approach is needed for deploying permission assistants in agentic systems, and Janus is publicly available to support future investigation into this dimension of agentic system design.

title: Disentangling Pictorial Cue Understanding from Language Bias in VLMs via Depth Ordering Task
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures, accepted to ECCV 2026 (30 pages, 13 figures, supplementary materials included)
summary: In this paper, we study depth perception of vision-language models (VLMs) to isolate the effects of pictorial depth cues and disentangle vision and language influences on model performance. To this end, we combine depth-ordering and odd-one-out psychophysical tasks: the VLMs are presented with images where one object is at different depth relative to other, otherwise identical, objects, and must determine whether the odd-one-out target is closer or farther to the observer. To create stimuli, we generate 2D views from simulated and real 3D scenes while controlling the presence of individual pictorial depth cues, enabling a fine-grained analysis of cue-level contributions. Language effects are examined by varying referring expression clarity. We also introduce a novel metric to quantify vision-vs-language sensitivities. Applying this methodology, we create the Odd-One-Out Depth (O3-D) dataset with 37K real and synthetic images and 147K image-question pairs. Evaluation of 12 open-source and commercial models on O3-D shows under-utilization of depth cues and depth-ordering accuracies between 47% and 56%, with no model above chance level. At the same time, our metric reveals strong linguistic bias in the answers. Neither chain-of-thought (CoT) nor in-context learning (ICL) significantly improves performance, suggesting that static image data alone may be insufficient for depth understanding. All code, the image generation pipeline, and the O3-D dataset are publicly released at https://github.com/lyiqian/o3-d.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates depth perception in vision-language models (VLMs) using psychophysical tasks with controlled pictorial depth cues, finding that models perform at chance level (47%-56%) and exhibit strong linguistic bias, with neither chain-of-thought nor in-context learning helping substantially.
AI.motivation: To isolate and quantify the contributions of pictorial depth cues vs. language biases in VLMs' depth perception abilities, addressing the lack of fine-grained analysis of cue-level understanding in current models.
AI.method: Combined depth-ordering and odd-one-out psychophysical tasks with controlled 2D views from simulated/real 3D scenes, manipulating individual pictorial depth cues and varying referring expression clarity; introduced a novel metric to quantify vision-vs-language sensitivities; created the O3-D dataset (37K images, 147K pairs).
AI.result: 12 VLMs achieved 47%-56% accuracy (none above chance); strong linguistic bias detected; CoT and ICL did not significantly improve performance; models under-utilize pictorial depth cues.
AI.conclusion: Static image data alone may be insufficient for robust depth understanding in VLMs, and current models rely more on linguistic cues than visual depth perception.

title: CoRe: Combined Rewards with Vision-Language Model Feedback for Preference-Aligned Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reward design remains a central challenge in reinforcement learning (RL). Hand-crafted rewards are often difficult to specify and may lead to suboptimal policies, while learned rewards from preferences can suffer from inefficiency and unstable training. Inspired by the dual nature of human learning explored in cognitive science, we decompose rewards into two complementary components: Formal Rewards (FR), explicitly designed based on task knowledge, and Residual Rewards (RR), learned from observations to capture implicit and nuanced preferences. Based on this decomposition, we propose CoRe, a hybrid framework that integrates FR and RR with vision-language models (VLMs) feedback to achieve preference-aligned policies without human involvement. Our contributions are twofold: (1) We propose a Formal Reward Module (FRM) that leverages VLMs to iteratively design and optimize FR based on task knowledge and preference feedback, enabling the continual improvement of policy during training; (2) We introduce a Residual Reward Module (RRM) that learns RR from video-level preference by employing VLMs to generate preference labels and capturing nuanced rewards that complement FR, ensuring alignment with human intent. Through the synergy of FRM and RRM, CoRe enables the automatic construction of reliable rewards that are efficient and preference-aligned. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoRe outperforms existing approaches in terms of policy learning effectiveness and efficiency on ten robotic manipulation tasks in simulation and five real-worlds. Videos can be found on our project website: https://core-2026.github.io/

AI.tldr: CoRe is a hybrid RL reward framework combining Formal Rewards (designed via VLMs) and Residual Rewards (learned from VLM-generated preference labels) to achieve efficient, preference-aligned policy learning without human involvement, outperforming baselines on robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Hand-crafted rewards are hard to specify and may yield suboptimal policies, while learned rewards from preferences suffer from inefficiency and unstable training. The authors aim to combine both approaches to overcome their respective limitations.
AI.method: CoRe decomposes rewards into Formal Rewards (FR) explicitly designed via VLMs based on task knowledge, and Residual Rewards (RR) learned from VLM-generated preference labels. The Formal Reward Module (FRM) iteratively designs/optimizes FR using VLMs, and the Residual Reward Module (RRM) learns complementary nuanced rewards from video-level preferences.
AI.result: CoRe outperforms existing methods in policy learning effectiveness and efficiency across ten simulated and five real-world robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The FR and RR synergy enables automatic construction of reliable, efficient, and preference-aligned rewards, eliminating the need for human involvement in reward design.

title: Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics Enable Complete and Scalable $\mathrm{E}(3)$-Equivariant Networks
summary: $\mathrm{E}(3)$-equivariant networks are promising for 3D atomistic system modeling, yet their scalability is limited by the $O(L^6)$ complexity of the Clebsch-Gordan Tensor Product (CGTP). The recently proposed Gaunt Tensor Product (GTP) reduces the complexity but is unable to capture the antisymmetric paths, resulting in incomplete expressivity. In this work, we present SpinGTP, an approach to overcome the GTP incompleteness by generalizing from scalar functions to Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics (SWSH). By relying on the algebraic properties of SWSH, SpinGTP recovers the missing antisymmetric interactions while maintaining the asymptotic efficiency of GTP. It also allows for a more expressive equivariant basis that naturally accounts for the parity-odd components of tensor products. We evaluate SpinGTP across diverse benchmarks, including Tetris, 3BPA, SPICE-MACE-OFF, and OC20. Our results show that SpinGTP achieves accuracies comparable to full CGTP. Notably, by explicitly capturing antisymmetric paths, SpinGTP exhibits superior performance in tasks involving chiral materials and non-centrosymmetric geometries. This work provides a complete, scalable, and mathematically rigorous path toward high-order equivariance in large-scale 3D atomistic system simulations.

AI.tldr: SpinGTP extends Gaunt Tensor Product with Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics to recover missing antisymmetric interactions, achieving full expressivity comparable to CGTP while maintaining GTP's asymptotic efficiency for 3D equivariant networks.
AI.motivation: E(3)-equivariant networks face scalability issues due to O(L^6) complexity of CGTP. While GTP reduces complexity, it cannot capture antisymmetric paths, leading to incomplete expressivity.
AI.method: SpinGTP generalizes from scalar functions to Spin-Weighted Spherical Harmonics (SWSH), leveraging their algebraic properties to recover antisymmetric interactions while maintaining GTP's efficiency. It also provides a more expressive equivariant basis accounting for parity-odd components.
AI.result: SpinGTP achieves accuracies comparable to full CGTP across Tetris, 3BPA, SPICE-MACE-OFF, and OC20 benchmarks. It shows superior performance on chiral materials and non-centrosymmetric geometries by explicitly capturing antisymmetric paths.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a complete, scalable, and mathematically rigorous path toward high-order equivariance in large-scale 3D atomistic system simulations.

title: Revisiting Chain-of-Thought Reasoning under Limited Supervision: Semi-supervised Chain-of-Thought Learning
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has emerged as an effective approach for activating latent reasoning capabilities in large language models. However, most existing CoT methods use reasoning chains mainly as inference-time prompts, while the generated reasoning traces are rarely reused as semi-supervised learning signals. In this report, we define \textbf{Semi-supervised Chain-of-Thought Learning} and propose \textbf{Semi-CoT}, a simple framework that uses unlabeled questions to construct pseudo reasoning supervision. Semi-CoT samples multiple pseudo-CoTs for each unlabeled question, estimates answer-level semantic entropy, and selects low-entropy reasoning chains as reliable pseudo-CoT demonstrations. This extends the self-training view of CoT from inference-time refinement to semi-supervised pseudo-supervision. Pilot experiments on AQuA, SVAMP, GSM8K, and MultiArith show that the entropy gate selects high-precision pseudo-CoTs, with pseudo-answer precision ranging from $91.36\%$ to $100\%$. Semi-CoT also gives small gains on SVAMP and GSM8K, while AQuA shows negative transfer and MultiArith reaches a ceiling. These results suggest that unlabeled questions can provide reliable pseudo reasoning signals, but their effective use still requires stronger demonstration selection or student training.

AI.tldr: Semi-CoT extends chain-of-thought reasoning by using unlabeled questions to generate pseudo reasoning chains, selecting low-entropy ones as training signals, with mixed results across math reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing CoT methods use reasoning chains mainly as inference-time prompts and rarely reuse generated reasoning traces as semi-supervised learning signals for model improvement.
AI.method: Semi-CoT samples multiple pseudo-CoTs for each unlabeled question, estimates answer-level semantic entropy, and selects low-entropy reasoning chains as reliable pseudo-CoT demonstrations for training.
AI.result: Entropy gate selects high-precision pseudo-CoTs (91.36%-100% precision). Small gains on SVAMP and GSM8K, negative transfer on AQuA, ceiling effect on MultiArith.
AI.conclusion: Unlabeled questions can provide reliable pseudo reasoning signals, but effective use requires stronger demonstration selection or improved student training methods.

title: Hidden-Shot: Towards One-Shot Task Generalization for Low-Level Vision Generalist Models
summary: Despite the intense engagement surrounding low-level vision generalist models, their effectiveness in zero/few-shot scenarios beyond learned tasks remains unverified. The primary challenge of developing an ideal generalist lies in achieving the ability to generalize from new unseen tasks, which also can be assessed by matched quantitative criteria. Existing methods have made some progress in prompt engineering but have not systematically explored this gap across a wide range of low-level visual tasks. Stimulated by the problem, we propose Hidden-Shot, an implicit prompt mechanism aimed at exploring low-level task adaptation in a vision generalist model. Specifically, the method extracts implicit visual task-based information, utilizes a global task-aware textural prompt, and selectively merges implicit information with in-task processing information to enhance one-shot capabilities in new tasks. The overall design performs direct injection in a cost-effective manner, while minimally altering the architecture of the original generalist model. Additionally, we introduce a data-driven evaluation framework termed C/U assessment to cover two basic scenarios, 3C4U (3 conventional and 4 unconventional tasks) for retraining existing models and 3C7U (3 conventional and 7 unconventional tasks) for training from scratch, as a comprehensive assessment to systematically test the generalization ability of low-level generalist models. Experiments on seven and ten datasets outperform the state-of-the-art vision generalist model, respectively verified by 3C4U and 3C7U framework. Our presented Hidden-Shot approach demonstrates superior performance on one-shot new tasks while maintaining consistent performance on existing tasks.

AI.tldr: Hidden-Shot proposes an implicit prompt mechanism for low-level vision generalist models that enhances one-shot generalization to unseen tasks, evaluated on new C/U benchmark frameworks (3C4U, 3C7U), outperforming state-of-the-art models.
AI.motivation: Existing low-level vision generalist models lack verified effectiveness in zero/few-shot generalization beyond learned tasks, and have not systematically explored this gap across a wide range of tasks with matched quantitative criteria.
AI.method: Hidden-Shot extracts implicit visual task-based information, uses a global task-aware textural prompt, and selectively merges implicit information with in-task processing information. It performs direct injection cost-effectively with minimal architectural changes to the generalist model.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art vision generalist models on both 3C4U (7 datasets) and 3C7U (10 datasets) evaluation frameworks, showing superior one-shot new task performance while maintaining existing task performance.
AI.conclusion: Hidden-Shot effectively achieves one-shot generalization for unseen low-level vision tasks through implicit prompting, and the C/U assessment framework provides comprehensive evaluation for low-level generalist models.

title: Lightweight Safe Reinforcement Learning for End-to-End UAV Navigation
summary: With the rapid development of autonomous aerial systems, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly deployed in applications such as inspection, environmental monitoring, and rescue, creating growing demand for reliable autonomous navigation. However, autonomous UAV navigation in dense environments remains challenging under sparse perception and dynamic constraints. Most reinforcement learning (RL) methods lack explicit safety mechanisms, leading to unsafe exploration, unstable training, and risky behaviors, especially during high-speed flight. Even in safe RL approaches, safety is often enforced by projecting policy outputs onto a safe action set, which may introduce instability. Meanwhile, many learning-based methods rely on dense inputs or large networks, increasing computational burden and limiting lightweight onboard deployment. Facing the above challenges, we propose a safety-constrained perception-control integrated framework for UAV navigation. A lightweight network encodes sparse observations into collision-risk-aware features using asymmetric and depthwise separable convolutions. We formulate the task as a constrained Markov decision process within a hierarchical control architecture and solve it using a Lagrangian-based safe PPO algorithm. Curriculum learning further improves training stability. Experiments with varying obstacle densities and flight speeds demonstrate higher success rates, improved safety, and better efficiency than existing reinforcement learning baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a safety-constrained perception-control integrated framework for UAV navigation, using a lightweight network with asymmetric/depthwise separable convolutions and a Lagrangian-based safe PPO algorithm with curriculum learning to achieve safer, more efficient autonomous flight in dense environments.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based UAV navigation methods lack explicit safety mechanisms, leading to unsafe exploration and risky behaviors in dense environments; additionally, many learning-based approaches rely on dense inputs or large networks, limiting lightweight onboard deployment.
AI.method: A lightweight network with asymmetric and depthwise separable convolutions encodes sparse observations into collision-risk-aware features; the task is formulated as a constrained Markov decision process within a hierarchical control architecture and solved using a Lagrangian-based safe PPO algorithm with curriculum learning.
AI.result: Experiments across varying obstacle densities and flight speeds demonstrate higher success rates, improved safety, and better efficiency compared to existing RL baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed safety-constrained framework effectively addresses challenges of unsafe exploration and computational burden, enabling reliable and efficient UAV navigation in dense environments.

title: The Rollout Infrastructure Tax in Coding-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Preprint. 6 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to ACM SoCC 2026
summary: Coding-agent reinforcement learning treats execution infrastructure as a background implementation detail, despite relying on large numbers of interactive software rollouts. This is a missed opportunity: measuring infrastructure overhead can reveal practical efficiency gains for RL post-training, where small per-rollout savings compound at scale. We present a comparative study of four execution substrates: single containers, hosted sandboxes, Kubernetes-orchestrated containers, and cloud virtual machines. We find up to $110\times$ variation in cold-start latency and a $1.8\times$ spread in projected worker-hours for one million 150-step trajectories. Our results suggest that future coding-agent RL systems should optimize execution substrates as part of the training system itself, not merely as deployment plumbing.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that the choice of execution infrastructure for coding-agent RL (containers, sandboxes, Kubernetes, VMs) causes up to 110× cold-start latency variation and 1.8× worker-hour spread at scale, arguing that infrastructure should be treated as a first-class component of the training system rather than just deployment plumbing.
AI.motivation: Current coding-agent RL systems treat execution infrastructure as a background detail, missing opportunities for efficiency gains at scale where small per-rollout savings compound significantly.
AI.method: Comparative study of four execution substrates: single containers, hosted sandboxes, Kubernetes-orchestrated containers, and cloud virtual machines.
AI.result: Up to 110× variation in cold-start latency and 1.8× spread in projected worker-hours for one million 150-step trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Future coding-agent RL systems should optimize execution substrates as part of the training system itself, not merely as deployment plumbing.

title: OPINE-World: Programmatic World Modeling with Ontology-error-Prioritized Interactive Exploration
summary: Learning how an environment behaves from interaction is central to building agents that adapt to unfamiliar tasks. World models learned with deep networks are flexible but data-hungry and transfer poorly beyond their training distribution. Program-synthesized world models, written as source code by LLMs and refined through counterexample-guided inductive synthesis (CEGIS), are instead data-efficient and reusable, yet they have been demonstrated mainly on structured-state worlds with a given object vocabulary, and a single program search does not scale to pixel-rendered environments whose object structure must be hypothesized flexibly. We introduce OPINE-World, an LLM agent that learns an object-centric programmatic world model online from interaction. OPINE-World couples two cooperating agents in a loop of hypothesis and test, one acting in the environment and one synthesizing the model in code with replay verification and model-based planning, and it steers exploration with a Bayesian measure of object-type adequacy we call ontology error. We evaluate OPINE-World on ARC-AGI-3, a benchmark for skill-acquisition efficiency in which the object vocabulary, the goal, and the action semantics are withheld. OPINE-World solves 20 of 25 games without per-game training and reaches an action-efficiency score of 78.4 against the human baseline.

AI.tldr: OPINE-World is an LLM agent that learns object-centric programmatic world models online from interaction, using two cooperating agents (actor and synthesizer) with Bayesian ontology error to guide exploration, achieving strong results on ARC-AGI-3 without per-game training.
AI.motivation: Deep network world models are data-hungry and transfer poorly beyond their training distribution, while existing program-synthesized world models work on structured-state worlds but don't scale to pixel-rendered environments where object structure must be flexibly hypothesized.
AI.method: OPINE-World couples two cooperating agents (actor and synthesizer) in a loop of hypothesis and test, using program synthesis with CEGIS, replay verification, model-based planning, and a Bayesian measure called ontology error to steer exploration.
AI.result: Solves 20 of 25 games on ARC-AGI-3 without per-game training, achieving an action-efficiency score of 78.4 against the human baseline.
AI.conclusion: Object-centric programmatic world models learned online via LLM-driven synthesis with ontology-guided exploration can scale to pixel-rendered environments and achieve efficient skill acquisition.

title: Boosting Infrared Small Target Detection via Logit-Domain Contrast and Adaptive Shape Refinement
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains challenging due to tiny target size, low signal-to-noise ratio, severe foreground-background imbalance, and blurred boundaries in complex scenes. Existing methods usually rely on post-activation probability-domain supervision for discrimination, where weak targets and strong clutter may produce saturated and close probabilities, limiting weak-target discrimination. Meanwhile, blurred boundaries and halo-like predictions mainly stem from thermal diffusion, tiny target scale, boundary uncertainty, and insufficient explicit contour constraints. To address these issues, we propose Adaptive-Contrastive SLSIoU (AC-SLSIoU), a plug-and-play discriminative and shape-aware loss for IRSTD. Specifically, a Logit-Domain Margin Constraint (LDMC) is introduced to enlarge the response gap between targets and informative hard negatives in the logit space, thereby enhancing weak-target discrimination. Adaptive Boundary Suppression (ABS) applies scale-aware annular penalties to refine target contours and suppress halo-like overflow responses. In addition, False-Alarm Focal Loss assigns larger weights to high-probability negative samples, further penalizing persistent high-confidence false alarms. Without introducing extra inference overhead, the proposed method can be seamlessly integrated into existing detectors and consistently improves both detection accuracy and shape quality. Extensive experiments and cross-backbone evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness, robustness, and generalization ability of the proposed method for infrared small target detection.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AC-SLSIoU, a plug-and-play loss function for infrared small target detection that enhances weak-target discrimination via logit-domain margin constraints, suppresses halo artifacts via adaptive boundary penalties, and reduces false alarms via focal loss weighting, improving both detection accuracy and shape quality without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing IRSTD methods suffer from saturated probabilities that limit weak-target discrimination (due to post-activation supervision), and produce blurred boundaries/halo predictions (due to thermal diffusion, tiny target scale, boundary uncertainty, and lack of explicit contour constraints).
AI.method: Three components: (1) Logit-Domain Margin Constraint (LDMC) — enlarges response gaps between targets and hard negatives in logit space; (2) Adaptive Boundary Suppression (ABS) — applies scale-aware annular penalties to refine contours; (3) False-Alarm Focal Loss — assigns larger weights to high-probability negatives to suppress false alarms. Integrated as a plug-and-play loss.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and cross-backbone evaluations show consistent improvements in detection accuracy and shape quality across existing detectors, with no extra inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: AC-SLSIoU is an effective, robust, and generalizable plug-and-play loss function that significantly improves IRSTD performance by addressing both weak-target discrimination and shape quality issues.

title: VLA-Corrector: Lightweight Detect-and-Correct Inference for Adaptive Action Horizon
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation models have recently achieved strong progress in embodied intelligence. To reduce policy-call frequency while preserving temporal coherence, most generative policies adopt an action chunk mechanism, executing multiple future actions in an open-loop manner under a fixed action horizon. However, this "predict-then-blindly-execute" paradigm sacrifices closed-loop reactivity: in contact-rich physical interactions, even small local perturbations can rapidly amplify within the open-loop blind spot, leading to compounding errors and ultimately task failure. To address this limitation, we propose VLA-Corrector, a lightweight corrective inference framework for action-chunked VLA policies. Without modifying the backbone policy weights, VLA-Corrector introduces a lightweight Latent-space Vision Monitor (LVM) that continuously compares predicted and actual visual feature evolution, enabling online detection of visual dynamics deviations. Once persistent deviation is detected, the system triggers a truncation event, discards the remaining stale actions, and invokes corrective replanning via Online Gradient Guidance (OGG). The detect-and-correct mechanism of VLA-Corrector naturally induces an event-triggered adaptive action horizon: it preserves long-horizon execution when the current chunk remains reliable, and invokes short-horizon corrective replanning when execution begins to drift. In doing so, VLA-Corrector mitigates the trade-off imposed by static horizons between execution robustness and policy-call frequency. It can be integrated into different VLA models without further retraining the VLA backbone, interrupting compounding errors while preserving much of the efficiency benefit of action chunking and substantially improving robustness in long-horizon, contact-rich robotic manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: VLA-Corrector is a lightweight corrective inference framework for action-chunked VLA policies that detects visual deviations online and triggers replanning, enabling adaptive action horizons without retraining the backbone model, improving robustness in contact-rich manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing action-chunked VLA policies sacrifice closed-loop reactivity due to open-loop execution, where local perturbations amplify in the blind spot, causing compounding errors and task failure in contact-rich physical interactions.
AI.method: VLA-Corrector introduces a Latent-space Vision Monitor (LVM) for online detection of visual dynamics deviations, and an Online Gradient Guidance (OGG) mechanism for corrective replanning when persistent deviation is detected, without modifying backbone policy weights.
AI.result: The detect-and-correct mechanism enables event-triggered adaptive action horizons, preserving long-horizon execution when reliable and invoking short-horizon replanning when drift occurs, improving robustness in long-horizon contact-rich manipulation.
AI.conclusion: VLA-Corrector mitigates the trade-off between execution robustness and policy-call frequency imposed by static horizons, integrates into different VLA models without retraining, and substantially improves robustness while preserving efficiency benefits of action chunking.

title: Conditional Inference Trees and Forests for Feature Selection
summary: Conditional inference trees (CIT) and conditional inference forests (CIF) reduce split-selection bias by testing features before choosing split thresholds, but repeated permutation tests and threshold searches can make these methods computationally expensive. We study CIT and CIF as top-$k$ feature-ranking methods for downstream prediction using real-data benchmarks, runtime ablations, and synthetic feature-recovery experiments. At a fixed node, if the features and permutation budget do not depend on the node responses, Bonferroni-corrected $+1$ Monte Carlo permutation $p$-values control nodewise rejection under the complete permutation null. CIF ranks 4th among 17 classification methods on 22 datasets and 3rd among 18 regression methods on 8 datasets. With Bonferroni correction held fixed, the CIF runtime ablations indicate that adaptive stopping and the number of thresholds searched have the largest measured effect on runtime: turning off adaptive stopping and using exact threshold search increase fitting time by 4.0--8.4$\times$ and 1.9--10.8$\times$, respectively, while downstream score changes are at most 0.011. Sparse high-$p$ simulations indicate that forest feature sampling can leave informative features out of many split decisions. Overall, the results support CIF as a top-$k$ feature-ranking method in the evaluated downstream prediction benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates conditional inference trees (CIT) and forests (CIF) as top-k feature-ranking methods, showing CIF performs competitively (4th/17 classification, 3rd/18 regression) while analyzing runtime trade-offs: adaptive stopping and threshold search have large effects on speed (up to 10.8× slowdown) with minimal accuracy loss (≤0.011).
AI.motivation: CIT and CIF reduce split-selection bias via permutation tests but are computationally expensive; the paper aims to systematically evaluate their effectiveness and efficiency as feature-ranking methods.
AI.method: The authors study CIT/CIF via real-data benchmarks, runtime ablations, and synthetic feature-recovery experiments, using Bonferroni-corrected Monte Carlo permutation p-values and analyzing the effects of adaptive stopping and threshold search on runtime.
AI.result: CIF ranks 4th among 17 classification methods on 22 datasets and 3rd among 18 regression methods on 8 datasets. Turning off adaptive stopping increases fitting time by 4.0-8.4×; exact threshold search increases it by 1.9-10.8×; downstream score changes are at most 0.011.
AI.conclusion: CIF is supported as a top-k feature-ranking method, though forest feature sampling can miss informative features in high-dimensional sparse settings.

title: Scaling Trends for Lie Detector Oversight in Preference Learning
summary: Deceptive behavior in LLMs is costly to monitor and prevent, motivating approaches such as Scalable Oversight via Lie Detectors (SOLiD) (Cundy & Gleave, 2025), which uses lie detectors to identify responses for review by high-cost labelers. In this paper, we scale SOLiD to larger models and evaluate it in more diverse and realistic preference-learning settings. We find favorable scaling: undetected deception drops from 34% for 1B-parameter models to 14% for 405B-parameter models at a detector true positive rate of 99%, and expensive human labelers can be removed entirely from the fine-tuning phase without a statistically significant increase in deception. However, SOLiD is sensitive to distribution shift between detector training and preference-training data, which can drive detector false positive rates to impractical levels.

AI.tldr: This paper scales SOLiD (lie detector-based oversight) to larger models (1B-405B parameters) in preference-learning settings. It finds deception decreases with scale (34% to 14% at 99% TPR) and human labelers can be removed without significant deception increase, but SOLiD is sensitive to distribution shift causing high false positive rates.
AI.motivation: Deceptive behavior in LLMs is costly to monitor and prevent, motivating efficient oversight approaches like SOLiD that use lie detectors to identify responses for selective human review.
AI.method: Scales SOLiD to larger models (1B-405B parameters) and evaluates in diverse preference-learning settings, including removing expensive human labelers from the fine-tuning phase.
AI.result: Undetected deception drops from 34% (1B) to 14% (405B) at 99% detector true positive rate. Human labelers can be removed without statistically significant increase in deception. However, SOLiD is sensitive to distribution shift between detector training and preference-training data.
AI.conclusion: SOLiD scales favorably to larger models, reducing deception effectively, but its sensitivity to distribution shift leading to impractical false positive rates remains a key limitation.

title: Mind the Gap: Standard 3DGS Evaluation Primarily Measures Near-Trajectory Interpolation
summary: Standard MipNeRF360-style 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) evaluation holds out every N-th frame -- but these frames have trained neighbors on both sides, so the metric measures near-trajectory interpolation rather than spatial generalization. We introduce a fair matched-count protocol that isolates this effect: both arms train on the same number of images and differ only in whether the holdout is spread evenly (interpolation) or forms a contiguous spatial sector (extrapolation). Our primary finding is a large, consistent interpolation-extrapolation gap of 3~12dB -- several times the differences typically reported between competing methods. The gap is robust to training noise, is in two cases large enough to flip a method ranking under multi-seed confirmation, and -- crucially -- persists across three representation families, including a non-Gaussian volumetric neural radiance field (NeRF), so it reflects spatial coverage rather than any one representation. Diagnostically, it is dominated by a diffuse/geometry-proxy component and tracks each view's angular distance to its nearest training view, a zero-cost signal that also guides capture planning; loss-side regularization yields only marginal gains. Standard holdouts remain useful for near-trajectory rendering but should not, alone, be read as evidence of spatial generalization. Prior work notes protocol sensitivity; ours is, to our knowledge, the first to combine matched-count paired holdout, cross-representation quantification, and a diagnostic analysis Table 1. We describe a spatial-holdout benchmark toolkit with standardized splits and baselines for 16 scenes, which we are preparing for public release.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals a large (3-12dB) interpolation-extrapolation gap in 3D Gaussian Splatting evaluation that is not explained by representation choice, training noise, or loss-side regularization. Standard holdouts measure near-trajectory interpolation, not true spatial generalization, and a matched-count protocol is introduced to isolate this effect across 16 scenes.
AI.motivation: Standard 3DGS evaluation (holding out every N-th frame) inadvertently measures near-trajectory interpolation because held-out frames have trained neighbors on both sides. This confounds true spatial generalization assessment, and the authors aim to isolate and quantify this effect.
AI.method: A matched-count protocol: both training arms use the same number of images, differing only in whether holdout frames are spread evenly (interpolation) or form a contiguous spatial sector (extrapolation). The gap is tested across three representation families (3DGS, NeRF, etc.), multiple seeds, and diagnostic analysis of angular distance to nearest training view.
AI.result: A consistent 3-12dB interpolation-extrapolation gap is found — several times larger than typical method differences. The gap flips method rankings in two cases under multi-seed confirmation, persists across all three representation families, and is dominated by a diffuse/geometry-proxy component tied to angular distance to nearest training view.
AI.conclusion: Standard holdouts are useful for near-trajectory rendering but should not be read as evidence of spatial generalization. The authors introduce a spatial-holdout benchmark toolkit with standardized splits and baselines for 16 scenes.
